evidence.text: Company: Meridian Home Health1
Website: https://meridianhomehealth.example
Industry: Healthcare Services
Question: Why is Meridian’s care quality improving while employee burnout and scheduling instability are getting worse?
Context:
Context:
Meridian Home Health provides in-home nursing, physical therapy, and elder care support across three regions.

Over the last two quarters, patient satisfaction scores increased from 82% to 91%. Families frequently mention that nurses are more attentive, care plans are clearer, and follow-up calls are more consistent.
At the same time, nurse turnover increased from 14% to 26%. Exit interviews repeatedly cite unpredictable schedules, emotional exhaustion, and frustration with last-minute route changes.
The operations team recently introduced a centralized scheduling dashboard. It improved visibility for managers, but field nurses say it made their days feel more tightly monitored and less flexible.
Regional managers report that they now catch missed visits faster, but they also spend more time intervening in daily scheduling conflicts.
The clinical quality team believes the new dashboard helped standardize follow-up routines and reduce missed care steps.
However, several senior nurses say quality improvements are coming from individual staff “absorbing the chaos,” not from healthier operations.
Dispatch coordinators say they are receiving more escalation messages than before because everyone can now see schedule gaps in real time.
The CEO is proud of the quality gains but worried the organization may be converting hidden labor into visible performance improvement.
One manager said, “The dashboard did not remove the work. It just made the work impossible to ignore.”
Another nurse said, “Patients are getting better care because we are catching everything, but it feels like there is no slack left in the system.”
Meridian is considering expanding into a fourth region next quarter, but leadership is unsure whether the current operating model can scale without worsening burnout.

nodes.description: Meridian Home Health provides in-home nursing, physical therapy, and elder care support across three regions.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: Over the last two quarters, patient satisfaction scores increased from 82% to 91%. Families frequently mention that nurses are more attentive, care plans are clearer, and follow-up calls are more consistent.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: At the same time, nurse turnover increased from 14% to 26%. Exit interviews repeatedly cite unpredictable schedules, emotional exhaustion, and frustration with last-minute route changes.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: The operations team recently introduced a centralized scheduling dashboard. It improved visibility for managers, but field nurses say it made their days feel more tightly monitored and less flexible.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: Regional managers report that they now catch missed visits faster, but they also spend more time intervening in daily scheduling conflicts.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: The clinical quality team believes the new dashboard helped standardize follow-up routines and reduce missed care steps.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: However, several senior nurses say quality improvements are coming from individual staff “absorbing the chaos,” not from healthier operations.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: Dispatch coordinators say they are receiving more escalation messages than before because everyone can now see schedule gaps in real time.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: The CEO is proud of the quality gains but worried the organization may be converting hidden labor into visible performance improvement.

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: One manager said, “The dashboard did not remove the work. It just made the work impossible to ignore.”

nodes.label: user
nodes.description: Another nurse said, “Patients are getting better care because we are catching everything, but it feels like there is no slack left in the system.”
